directions.name: Retune PTX Hot-Band Group Traversal Around The 6-Row Window
directions.hypothesis: The move from the regressed non-PTX sibling back to the PTX microkernel recovered DRAM and runtime, so the live opportunity is now inside the grouped-row mapping itself rather than in broad kernel-family swaps. Keeping the mid-width 6-row window but changing how `physical_pid` is translated into `logical_block_x/logical_block_y` should preserve the recovered memory locality while reducing the residual long-scoreboard cost that still trails the earlier PTX baseline.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Hot-band orchestration locality and CTA traversal order inside the PTX microkernel, visible as long-scoreboard pressure with DRAM and L2 acting as guardrails.
directions.risk: Low to medium. This stays inside the recovered PTX family and does not reopen a closed broad branch, but the upside may be limited if the remaining loss is no longer driven by CTA ordering.
directions.stop_condition: Stop this line if one bounded traversal change keeps DRAM/L2 near the current run but still does not reduce long-scoreboard or improve runtime over 25.76332855 ms.

directions.name: Trim PTX Export Syncs Without Reopening The Paired-Scratch Family
directions.hypothesis: The current PTX path already restored the single-stage zero-padding export scratch, but the helper still executes a `__syncwarp()` for every tile as it walks the 64x64 export recursion. A smaller helper rewrite that batches or reorders the per-row export inside the existing single-stage scratch could cut scoreboard and sync overhead without reintroducing the two-stage paired-scratch variant that already measured negative.
directions.expected_bottleneck: PTX export-side synchronization and shared-to-global store orchestration in the hot-band epilogue.
directions.risk: Medium. The surface is still bounded, but it is close to a previously closed family, so the implementation must avoid silently recreating the paired-lifetime path that already regressed.
directions.stop_condition: Abandon this family if the helper cleanup raises DRAM or barrier cost or drifts back toward the closed paired-export behavior without a runtime gain.

directions.name: Retune PTX Prefetch Handoff In The K Loop
directions.hypothesis: With the PTX path restored and registers unchanged, the remaining gap may come from when the future tile is staged rather than from CTA grouping alone. A bounded retime of the `cp.async` wait/commit handoff or the A/B staging order in the PTX microkernel can test whether the lingering long-scoreboard is a feed-latency problem instead of a traversal problem.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Copy-pipeline depth and handoff timing in the PTX hot-band steady-state loop.
directions.risk: Medium. This is a real alternative family, but a previous non-PTX cadence retime was weak, so the gain here depends on PTX-specific overlap rather than generic two-stage timing.
directions.stop_condition: Stop after one bounded retime if the stall mix merely shifts between barrier and scoreboard without improving runtime.

notes: Round 1/17 diagnosis for run 20260420_153144_bf16_gemm_v1_c550d79. Human-review mapping: accept narrow PTX-adjacent follow-ups on top of the restored zero-padding PTX baseline; defer pure baseline-restore work because the loop already anchored a new accepted base at 25.76332855 ms; reject reopening broad default-promotion, staged K32, paired export-lifetime, and helper-flattening families. The latest run recovered the PTX path and pulled DRAM back down to 11.52%, but it still trails the earlier PTX baseline because long-scoreboard rose to 7.79 while tensor activity stayed flat near 48%. That makes grouped traversal the best next move, with export-helper cleanup and PTX-specific prefetch retiming as the two secondary families.
